Title: Air Pollution as a Causal Driver of Cardiovascular and Systemic Inflammation

Abstract:
Long-term exposure to particulate air pollution causes systemic inflammation, increasing
cardiovascular and respiratory disease risk. This meta-analysis of 23 studies (n = 890,000)
quantifies the causal effect of PM2.5 on inflammatory markers and downstream disease outcomes.

Introduction:
The cardiovascular effects of air pollution are mediated through systemic inflammatory
pathways. Particulate matter enters the bloodstream via pulmonary absorption and directly
causes endothelial inflammation. This inflammation subsequently accelerates atherosclerosis
and increases cardiovascular disease risk.

Key Findings:
1. PM2.5 exposure (per 10 μg/m³ increase) caused a 12% increase in CRP levels
   (pooled OR: 1.12, 95% CI: 1.08-1.17).

2. Systemic inflammation caused by air pollution mediated 45% of the cardiovascular
   disease risk attributable to pollution.

3. The causal pathway was confirmed through Mendelian randomization: genetic variants
   associated with higher residential pollution exposure showed the same inflammatory
   and cardiovascular outcomes.

4. Air pollution causes endothelial dysfunction through oxidative stress mechanisms,
   leading to increased arterial stiffness.

5. Long-term air pollution exposure causes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
   through persistent airway inflammation, consistent with findings in cohort studies.

Discussion:
The evidence for a causal rather than correlational relationship between air pollution
and inflammation is now robust. Natural experiments (policy changes reducing pollution)
demonstrate that reductions in PM2.5 cause corresponding reductions in inflammatory
markers within 6-12 months.

Inflammation serves as the critical hub connecting air pollution exposure to multiple
downstream disease outcomes including respiratory disease, cardiovascular disease,
and metabolic syndrome.

Conclusion:
Air pollution is a causal driver of systemic inflammation, which mediates its effects
on multiple organ systems. Reducing pollution exposure causes measurable improvements
in inflammatory profiles and downstream health outcomes.
